item.title: How I feel about this now, nearly five years later
item.content:encoded:
I think Stephen King's quote on Carrie (his debut novel) sums up my feelings well:

In retrospect, it reminds me of a cookie baked by a first grader — tasty enough, but kind of lumpy and burned on the bottom.

This is not perfect - it's overly long, awkward where it doesn't need to be, and reflects my struggles in writing realistic dialogue. But it gave me the confidence to follow with the five years worth of SCPs I have written since this.

This is my Ur work - Ur-bserver, if you'll forgive the horrible pun. I wouldn't put this on a pedestal as what I'm capable of as a writer, but compare it side-by-side with SCP-2135 (mid-career) and SCP-3185 (recent) and it's definitely proof I've gotten better over the years.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Three years later I am sober and find this quite a good sympathetic ghost. :)

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Borrowing ideas is fine. Stealing text is a problem. If your stuff is not copied word-for-word from elsewhere, I don't imagine you'd have anything to worry about.

Keep in mind though, I'm not staff atm, so if they decided to tell me I'm wrong, listen to them instead.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Inspired by both our recent plagiarism scandal and the fact that this was translated into Russian a few days ago, I'd like to clarify a few sources of inspiration I had for this article.

This was my first successful submission, and I hadn't had much experience in writing successful SCPs at the time I posted this. Because I was still learning, I drew on other, more successful stories I had enjoyed previously. Specifically, these two inspired much of the article:

1. SCP-1510. Should go without saying, but the idea of an ancient person talking to modern people through…something…was appealing to me. I love the idea of ancient perspectives on modern things, so I adapted this somewhat for the article.

2. This scene from the video game Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic. KOTOR is my favorite game ever, and Ajunta Pall is a tragic figure who stuck with me long after I finished the game. SCP-1395-A is largely based on him; the line about "ancient" being used to describe him is adapted into the story.

Obviously I think both of these fall under "inspiration" rather than "plagiarism," else I never would've posted the SCP in the first place. But I just wanted to make this known in case somebody looked up the source material and thought I was stealing ideas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: upvote from me, i like the idea and how its written. first one ive seen in a while dealing with gods and all that without being something that will eventually enslave humanity or destroy the world. The "whats at the back of the cave" factor is just high enough imo

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do not doubt you're 100% correct. Thing is, this guy isn't stuck in an ancient time. He's been there since he was cursed. So I imagine he has changed, even if he doesn't know it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Replaced the original image with a Creative Commons image, taken by one Andy Arthur and available here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly what wishun said- if the people who explored the cave saw nothing unusual but then saw this guy on their camera/heard him, they would be pretty freaked out- and maybe the video even got on the web, too. If so, the author should add in a more clarified section- something along the lines of an agent in the Greek government seeing a video about a ghost cave, and then the Foundation takes it offline or debunks it somehow. That would really make this better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was originally just "Greek", but I changed it to Athic during the revisions because someone said it would be more historically plausible. Feel free to change it if you think it sounds better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted with a few reservations - namely the dialogue in the interview log, which I think could do toning down slightly, and the lack of a sense of grounding for why -A is strongly resisting people going further into the cave when he makes no indication of this in the log. Also, 'Athic Greek'? I am almost certain this should read 'Attic' Greek, though I've held off on fixing because Carystus didn't actually speak Attic Greek (instead speaking the Ionic dialect), so possible the author knows something I don't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe some spelunkers had a camcorder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think the interview log needs to be that long.

I also have been trained to believe that, "Oh man, you just wouldn't understand," is bad. The thing about the end of the article that I think is inconsistent, is that the Foundation suspending testing as presented takes place as a result of the doctor saying that.

If you're going for the big underlying creepy that shall not be named, I don't think you're currently showing enough to pull off that twist at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One small point- 'Christ' is closely derived from 'Christos', which means 'Anointed' in ancient Greek. So the chap in the cave would probably understand the term even if he doesn't know of the individual it refers to.

Also in general his dialogue is kind of hokey. I don't think anyone in history has ever really spoken like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Which is actually the thing that bothered me most about this, and keeping it from really grabbing me.At this point all we know is that we should be afraid of whats in the cave, because you told us it was scary. That doesn't do much for me, nor does it have anything to do with the 1395 except share the same cave.

What happens when someone goes deeper into the cave has nothing to do with him or how he got there, at least according to his story- that is, it's not like he became what he was by venturing too deeply. I think providing some further link between the cave interior and his condition would improve this, while still maintaining the mystery.

In my head, whatever made 1395 what he is is somehow in that cave, and if the Doctor had gone further he would have joined him. This makes 1395 even more tragic because he is trying to save others even though it would mean someone to talk to. But, what if he he wasn't worried about someone joining him, but is worried the Foundation would stop whatever is keeping him alive? Maybe he is doing the same thing with the Doctor that he did with the beggar, looking out for number one.

I dunno, I think the article would have that extra pull if the [REDACTED] implied more than just mystery + ambiguous fear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the whole thing, except for the end where they went far in to the cave. Pretty much all of it was redacted, and really, the idea of some serious, frightening event that's been erased from the records is quite overdone. I feel like it would have been better overall without the ending part.

Still, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And the humans! I mean, they often don't even let us the time to explain the situation.

"sorry Zeus, I'd like to _ _ _ _ but I-"

PUNISHMENT!

" have AIDS… well shit, I'm a spider now!"

Probably happened thousands of times.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this SCP. It's weird, a little creepy, and I feel little sorry for the poor guy. It's well written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It has to do with what happened to the Doctor behind the [REDACTED]. Without giving too much away, he saw what would happen if they ventured further into the cave, and what he saw horrified him beyond belief. So 1395-A is OK with people visiting him, but he doesn't want them poking deeper into his home because of what it'll do to them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wondering if I'm missing something. The ghost seems to have a sudden personality change, from wanting company, to not letting people "join him".

Is there a reason to this that I'm not seeing? Or is that just how its supposed to be?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted because I like the concept, though I think it could use some streamlining. The interview sort of goes on and on without really adding much mystery or anything interesting IMO. In fact it sort of nonchalantly explains away a good chuck of mystery with the old man and the curse. I wonder if it would be stronger to cut the interview down and make it that the guy can't remember what happened to him as well, or something about his past he can't directly talk about that ties into why people don't remember talking with him. Possibly change it so that every time people leave he also forgets the conversation?

I like this tortured soul trapped in a cave and cursed to be forgotten, it's got more meat on it than most ghost stories, but it may benefit from hinting at things more than explaining his history all out in the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm in with the "it doesn't do anything for me" crowd. Can't really explain why (too drunk).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, no. Really though, it's a solid start. Looking forward to more from you in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On a side note, this SCP was inspired by both 1510 and this scene from Star Wars: KOTOR where the player character meets the spirit of Ajunta Pall. I liked the idea of a tormented spirit, and added the amnesia to make him more lonely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I was reading this SCP, I was hoping that it would be a reference to Plato's cave allegory. It would be very cool if this SCP would be connected in some way with Plato. That would make it waaay more creepy: the cave really existing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: By popular request, I've removed the addendum explaining its origins.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm assuming the implication is that this is Pan. Scaring the early visitors and "I am not some trickster God" both point that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The dialog just seemed to have some sticking points that ruined believability for me… but I still like this one. It isn't great, but I found it charming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, I can see that as outside observors noticing that when their friend Bob went exploring in that cave, he came out with no memory of doing so, then ascribing it to a haunted/unlucky/evil/etc. cave.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How can their be rumors of a 'ghost cave' if no one remembers? Keep in mind, you don't HAVE to explain how something is found, unless it adds to the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like I'm missing a reference and -A's manner of talking feels unbelievably unnatural. Overall, it's just not interesting for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I totally agree. Also, I'm fine with amnesiac/amnestic being interchangeable when referring to the little pills we give people, but you can't have an "amnesiac effect." That just doesn't make sense. You actually need it to say "amnestic" there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You definitely did take the feedback given to you, though I feel the new addendum can be expanded upon. Neutral vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to upvote it, but…

This is inconsistent! From the description:

Following the encounter, SCP-1395-A will demanifest, erasing all memories of the encounter from the individual(s)' mind.

If that's the case, then how is it that

SCP-1395 was discovered in 19██ after reports of a "ghost cave" reached the Greek government.

If no one can remember their encounters with the shade, how could the Greek government have known about it? How could the Foundation have decided it was important to continue investigating it? It doesn't make any sense, and not in the good way.

I'm down-voting for the present. If you can fix this error and make it convincing within 24 hrs, I'm sure that will be enough to turn my vote around.

E: I've rescinded my -1 since this was first posted, but without actually incorporating the justification into the SCiP, it does not yet warrant a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It may be the Greek mythology nerd in me, but I liked it. Some of the language in the interview felt a little artificial, but then again he's told the story over and over again. I think what sets it apart for me is the idea of an anomaly being happy to be contained by the Foundation, if only for the company…I'm a sucker for the softer side of the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's amazing how much of Greek mythology is devoted to the gods being dicks to each other.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Greek gods can be such bitches.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough, on both accounts. I'll hold off on tagging in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If all you're going to do is put the two most basic tags in, then don't do it at all. It doesn't hurt a page to be untagged for a few hours, and putting any tag in will take it off certain watchlists.

As for the SCP itself… it's not badly written, per se, but it's just not doing it for me. It's a fairly predictable ghost story about someone who is suffering karmic justice, and the "you can't hope to understand" bit at the end just killed it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Appreciate help with the anachronisms :) the article feels better now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is pretty good. It kept my interest until the end and it isn't implausible or absurd. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A bunch of historical innaccuracies were fixed this morning by working with the author.

I like this one honestly. It's not the most flashy effect, but it fits the tone of the site better than quite a few. It's anomalous, we contain it. It's not necessarily trying to murder you.

Upvote for Greek History.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I moved around the redacted and added in some stuff. Any improvement?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Instead of [he pauses], it should be [SCP-1395-A pauses] when dr. Somebody is talking with 1395 of how it became in is present state.

I'm sleepy, even though its 9:35 in the morning at London so excuse me if I'm unclear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The writing is solid, but I'm reminded too much of SCP-1510 for this to have any sort of impact. If something really cool was behind that [REDACTED] in the addendum that would set it apart from the crowd, then it could work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After some feedback from IRC and the draft forum, I've tweaked this a bit and hopefully made it interesting. Feedback is appreciated. :)
